title: Tutorial 1
text:
Soft corals growing on a pier, Indonesia | Photo Credit: Erik Lucas


In this class, we will be using a suite of packages known as the tidyverse. These packages were originally developed by Hadley Wickham and Garrett Grolemund as a new approach to using R. Since it’s widespread use and continued development, the tidyverse has made R more user friendly and more flexible for the purposes of data science. By the end of this tutorial, my goal is to familiarize you with the tidy workflow and the commands that will allow you to work with your own data.
To complete this tutorial, use the “R Prep.rmd” file located on the class canvas page. This tutorial will go between learning skills with examples and setting up the questions in that assignment. Your R Preparedness assignment is due August 27th prior to our first data workshop day.



Be able to manually import a .csv file.
Understand variables, vectors, and logical operators.
Explore, filter, and transform using dplyr.
Generate plots with ggplot.




For this tutorial you are expected to already know how to create a new .rmd file, get your working directory, and set your working directory. If you are not able to do so, return to Tutorial 0 for a quick review on these skills.






Important



To start, ensure your R-Prep.rmd file is in your Coding Tutorials folder. If you do not have a coding tutorials folder, make a folder path on your computer like the following: /yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials.
Alongside answering assignment questions in the R-Prep assignment, I would also like you to create your own Rmarkdown file called tutorial-1.rmd to follow along as you work through this tutorial. Do not do work for your R Preparedness assignment in your tutorial-1.rmd file. Instead use this file as a way to test your skills and practice your code.
Write your final answers for the assignment is the R-Prep.rmd only. Do not submit the tutorial-1.rmd as the submission for your R Preparedness assignment.


In this tutorial we will be using the package palmerpenguins as well as an example data set provided on the class canvas page. Palmer penguins is a CRAN data set provided that is a better alternative to the iris data set. If you’d like to know more about why we won’t be using iris in this course, you can read more here.
Palmer penguins is a data set including size measurements, clutch observations, and blood isotope ratios for adult foraging Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo penguins observed on islands in the Palmer Archipelago near Palmer Station, Antarctica.
First, install palmerpenguins into your local environment by using the command install.packages().

install.packages("palmerpenguins")

When installing multiple packages at once, you will use something known as a character vector combine ( c() ) followed by the list of packages you’re installing.

install.packages(c("package1", "package2", "package3", "package4"))

The packages are now installed, but not currently active in our library. To make the packages usable and active in our current session, we use the library() command.

library(palmerpenguins)







Tip



To keep things neat and tidy, I suggest using one R chunk to complete your “setup” (i.e., package installation, library imports, and other global options you set in your script). It would look something like this in your .rmd file:

# gloabl chunk options (example)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

# getting and setting working directory. Always double check if this worked
getwd()
setwd("/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 1")
getwd()

# install packages 
install.packages(c("package1", 
                   "package2", 
                   "package3", 
                   "package4")
                 )

# load multiple packages at once
my_packages <- c("package1", # perhaps note what this package is used for
                   "package2", # bwt these are not real packages
                   "package3", # don't try to actually install them
                   "package4") # (HINT: it won't work)
lapply(my_packages, library, character.only = TRUE)



Try it yourself: Create a new chunk and name it “data import”. In this class, we will save our data sets as .csv files (comma separated files). As scientists, we may use Excel or Google Sheets to input data, but it is best practice to save individual data sets as .csv files for simple, easy importing. To read a .xls file into R, you would need to install a third party package which adds an additional computing step during set up.
Navigate to our class canvas page. Go to Files and open the Coding Tutorials folder. Download the metadata_tidepool.csv file and save it in your Coding Tutorials folder. If you’ve properly set your working directory and saved the file to the correct location, this next step will be easy.
Import your .csv data set using the read.csv() command and saving it in a data frame object called ‘data’.

data <- read.csv("metadata_tidepool.csv", header = TRUE)

The argument header = TRUE will ensure that when you import your .csv, R is treating the first row of your data as column names. You would set this as header = FALSE if your data set did not contain column names.
Great, now we have two different data sets to play around with. Let’s start with Palmer penguins.






Question 1
1a. Get and set your working directory based on the suggested file path in Tutorial 0.
1b. Install the Palmer penguins data set onto your computer.
1c. Import the tidepool metadata practice data set.






It’s good practice to ensure that when you import a new data frame into your computing environment, you ensure everything looks approximately as it should (i.e., variable names are included and correct, variable types seem accurate to your data, and number of observations is approximately accurate).
This is simply done with three commands that I suggest you always run when first importing data into R: summary(), head(), and tail().

summary(penguins)

head() returns the first parts of a vector, matrix, table, data frame, or function. tail() returns the last parts of the object.






Question 2
2a. Retrieve the summary, head, and tail of the tide pool metadata set.
2b. What’s the mean tidepool volume?
2c. How many rows does the tidepool metadata set have?






Looking at the summary of penguins reveals this is an 8 variable data frame: 3 categorical variables (species, island, sex), 4 numeric variables (bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g), and 1 integer variable (year).
To check what kind of variable is contained within a data frame object, you use the class() command.

# syntax example of class()
class(dataframe$variableofinterest)

The command is broken down as class(x$variable) where x is the data frame object you’re inspecting, $ is the extraction operator of x, and variable is simply the name of the variable you’re extracting from x.

class(penguins$species)

[1] "factor"


This returns the class of the variable species extracted from the penguins data frame. You should see “factor” in your own .rmd, like above.
It’s important you understand and can differentiate between the atomic data types you might encounter when using R. However, for this class and likely beyond, you’ll only really need to focus on the first four.










Type
Description
Example Value
class() Output
Common Use Case




Numeric
Default for all real numbers; Can be continuous or discrete.
10 or 2.1415
“numeric”
Continuous measurements, math calculations


Integer
Whole numbers explicitly defined.
7L or year
“integer”
Discretely counted numbers, IDs, loops, dates


Character
Text strings wrapped in single or double quotes, categorical variables with no numerical value.
“hello” or “yes” or “female”
“factor”
Names, descriptive labels, text data


Logical
Boolean states representing truth values.
TRUE and/or FALSE
“logical”
Filtering data, evaluating conditions


Complex
Numbers containing real and imaginary components.
3 + 2i
“complex”
Purely mathematical or signal functions.


Raw
Unprocessessed binary data stored ad individual bytes.
as.raw(43)
“raw”
Reading binary files or protocol streams.



In R, we have values (the data), vectors (the container), and variables (the names).
Values in R must belong to an atomic data type (like the six types above).
Vectors are an ordered collection of values. Vectors are homogeneous, meaning all values inside a single vector must be of the same type.

To create a vector you combine values using the c() (combine) function.
If you mix data types, R forces lower types into higher types (Logical < Integer < Double < Character) without throwing an error.

For example, c(5, "Hello") forces the numeric 5 to become a factor "5".

Access specific elements of a vector by using square brackets []. R uses 1-based indexing, so the first element is at position 1.


# create a numeric vector
ages <- c(15, 20, 25, 30)

# extracting the second element follows 1-based idexing
ages[2] # should return 20

[1] 20


Variables are the “labeled boxes” of your data. When you write code, you use the assignment operator <- to bind a name (the variable) to a data object (like a vector).

While the equals sign = works for assignment, <- is preferred (and the widely recognized standard for assignment).
Variable names are case- and space-sensitive (my_data is different from My_Data or my_ data).
Assigning a new value to an existing variable overwrites its previous contents.


fruits <- c("apple", "cherry", "blueberry")
print(fruits)

[1] "apple"     "cherry"    "blueberry"



# assigning a new value will un-write the previously assigned content of "fruits"
fruits <- c("lettuce", "carrots", "celery") 
print(fruits)

[1] "lettuce" "carrots" "celery" 


We use the print() function to give the output or generate the contents of a vector or object into the R Console.
All R statements where you create objects , assignment statements, have the same form: object_name <- value. Again, don’t use =. It’s just confusing.






Tip



Getting tired of continuously, manually typing <- ? Well, you’re just in luck!! Statisticians and those who use R agree with that sentiment, so there’s actually a keyboard shortcut! Just type Alt + - and voila! You’re very own assignment argument.
This is also great because R automatically gives you spaces on both sides of the assignment arrow, which (again) is good coding practice and etiquette. Code is miserable to read on a good day, so giveyourselfabreakand use spaces.


This harkens to the code etiquette tab, but please ensure you’re giving your assigned objects, vectors, data frames (or literally everything else) a descriptive name. In this course, I’d like you to use snake_case, where you will separate lowercase words of a named object with _.

i_use_snake_case
otherPeopleUseCamelCase
some.people.use.periods
And_aFew.People_RENOUNCEconvention.like_fre_AKS

Sometimes the data you have doesn’t match your coding conventions. Our tide pool meta data mixes snake_case and camelCase. This is a very common issue and often happens with data sets that include multiple contributors.
It is best practice to agree and stick to a single convention when collaborating. To maintain continuity, you can rename variables using your agreed-upon convention.
For us, this convention is snake_case. To do this, you can use dplyr or base R. We’ll learn more about dplyr in a moment, but for now lets learn how to do this in base R.

# explicit name matching in base R to re-name data frame variables
names(data) [names(data) == "Old_Column_Name"] <- "new_column_name"

####OR####

# rename ALL columns all at once
names(data) <- c("column_1", "column_2", "column_3", "column_4")







Question 3
3a. What is the atomic data type of tide pool size? How many tide pool sizes are there?
3b. Rename the tidepool metadata object (if you haven’t already) using snake_case convention.
3c. Rename all variables in your new tide pool data frame to match snake_case conventions.






If you’ve noticed the double semi-colon after certain names of things (e.g., knitr:: , ggplot:: , dplyr::) this is an easy indication I am talking about a package.
When installing packages that overwrite previously installed or loaded packages in R, but you still want to use those functions, you’ll need to specify what package relates to what function. The two most common conflicts are two functions from the stats:: package (which is pre-loaded in base R):

stats::filter()

stats::lag()

If you receive conflict messages when running filter() or lag(), it’s likely because you’ve overwritten one or the other with either stats:: or dplyr::. Use the package that correlates to the command you’re trying to execute by indicating so with ::.


To transform our data, we will be using the dplyr package, which is a small component of the tidyverse package suite. If you haven’t done so before, install and load the tidyverse and dplyr packages in your setup chunk.

install.packages(c("tidyverse", "dplyr"))

# load multiple packages at once
CRAN_packages <- c("tidyverse", "dplyr")
lapply(CRAN_packages, library, character.only = TRUE)

CRAN packages are add-on modules written for R by other programmers. lapply() applies your vector CRAN_packages using the library() function, ensuring R passes your string of variables (your packages) in your vector as actual package names R will recognize. This allows you to load multiple packages at once without having to retype library() over and over.






Question 4
Use code to install the tidyverse and dplyr packages; Load the packages into your working session.






There are five key functions that make dplyr extraordinarily valuable for data visualization and transformation.

Pick observations by their values using filter()
Reorder rows with arrange()
Pick variables by their names with select()
Create new variables with functions of existing variables using mutate()
Collapse many values down to a single summary with summarize()

These commands are often used in conjunction with group_by(), which changes the scope of each function by going group-by-group, if desired. All of these functions together provide the verbage of data manipulation. All of the verbs (functions) work similarly where:

Argument one is the data frame.
Subsequent arguments describe what you’d like to actually do with the data.
The result is saved in a new data frame.

Do these things and you can successfully manipulate any data frame.



filter() subsets observations based on their values followed by a logical operator that you provide. For example, say I want to only look at Chinstrap penguin measurements from 2007.

filter(penguins, species == "Chinstrap", year == 2007)

# A tibble: 26 × 8
   species   island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
 1 Chinstrap Dream            46.5          17.9               192        3500
 2 Chinstrap Dream            50            19.5               196        3900
 3 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          19.2               193        3650
 4 Chinstrap Dream            45.4          18.7               188        3525
 5 Chinstrap Dream            52.7          19.8               197        3725
 6 Chinstrap Dream            45.2          17.8               198        3950
 7 Chinstrap Dream            46.1          18.2               178        3250
 8 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          18.2               197        3750
 9 Chinstrap Dream            46            18.9               195        4150
10 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          19.9               198        3700
# ℹ 16 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>


dplyr functions never permanently modify their inputs, so if you want to save the result, you’ll need to use an assignment <- operator.

chinstrap_2007_df <- filter(penguins, species == "Chinstrap", year == 2007)

R will either print out the results if you explicitly ask using print(), or save them into a variable without printing. If you want to print and save it without writing an extra line of code, wrap the entire argument in parentheses.

(chinstrap_2007_df <- filter(penguins, species == "Chinstrap", year == 2007))

To filter effectively, you need to understand how to correctly select comparison and logical operators. R gives you quite a few:

>, greater than
>=, greater than or equal to
<, less than
<=, less than or equal to
!=, not equal to
==, equal to

Boolean operators combine comparisons and logical operators when you’d like to filter multiple things at once. This is where filtering is the most confusing; Even after 4 years of using dplyr consistently, I am still struggling to understand this. So, I am under no expectation that you are to master this, I am simply giving you this information, and you may do with it what you’d like.
Boolean Operations are as follows:

& is “and”
| is “or”
! is “not”


Say, for example, I’d like to find penguins that were measured in 2007 or 2008.

filter(penguins, year == 2007 | year == 2008)

This reads in plain English as “find all penguins measured in year 2007 or year 2008”. You can also simplify if you’re filtering the data frame by the same variable by using x %in% y.

filter(penguins, year %in% c(2007,2008))

Or for character variables…

filter(penguins, species %in% c("Chinstrap", "Adelie"))

What if I’d like to do more complicated sub-setting? For example, I’d like to filter for every penguin that isn’t a Adelie and Chinstrap penguin and that have bills <40mm.

filter(penguins, !(species %in% c("Adelie", "Chinstrap") & bill_length_mm > 40))

When filtering, you can simplify by remembering that:

!(x & y) is the same as !x & !y
!(x | y) is the same as !x & !y




Filtering and using comparison operators is often tricky when values are missing. NA represents an unknown value. Unknown values are contagious, and any operation or command that requires all values won’t run when values are missing. For example:

# let x be Penelope's age. Sadly, we have no idea how old Penelope is.
x <- NA

# let y be Jack's age. We also don't know how old Jack is. Bummer.
y <- NA

# Are Jack and Penelope the same age?
x == y

[1] NA

# We don't know because Jack and Penelope's ages are NA's!!!!!

If you’d like to know if a value is missing, use is.na().

is.na(x)

[1] TRUE


Filtering data will only include rows where the condition of is.na() == FALSE.

fake_data <- tibble(x = c(1, NA, 3))
filter(fake_data, x > 1)

# A tibble: 1 × 1
      x
  <dbl>
1     3


But perhaps we’d like to include all rows, even if they have NAs, then you’d explicitly ask R to do so.

filter(fake_data, is.na(x) | x > 1)

# A tibble: 2 × 1
      x
  <dbl>
1    NA
2     3

5b. Filter the Palmer penguins data set to only include male penguins that were measured in every year except 2007 on Biscoe island (HINT: try using the ! logical operator to exclude 2007).

Above, we’ve arranged penguin observations by largest bill length, further sorted by largest bill depth. See what happens to the first few rows when we instead sort by bill_depth_mm first.

Question 6
6a. How could you use arrange() to sort all missing values to the start? (HINT: try using is.na()).
6b. In the tide pool data frame, arrange our observations in descending order by the Macro-organism Shannon mean and the Macro-organism richness.






Its not uncommon to receive data sets with hundreds or maybe even thousands of variables. Working with a data frame that dense is computationally expensive, so sub-setting down to the variables of interest is best practice when dealing with data like that. select() gives you the power to accomplish that.

# select columns of interest
select(penguins, species, island, sex, year)

# A tibble: 344 × 4
   species island    sex     year
   <fct>   <fct>     <fct>  <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen male    2007
 2 Adelie  Torgersen female  2007

10 Adelie  Torgersen <NA>    2007

 8 Adelie  Torgersen           39.2          19.6               195
 9 Adelie  Torgersen           34.1          18.1               193
10 Adelie  Torgersen           42            20.2               190
# ℹ 334 more rows

# select all columns except those from species to flipper_length_mm
select(penguins, -(species:flipper_length_mm))

# A tibble: 344 × 3
   body_mass_g sex     year
         <int> <fct>  <int>
 1        3750 male    2007
 2        3800 female  2007
 3        3250 female  2007
 4          NA <NA>    2007
 5        3450 female  2007
 6        3650 male    2007
 7        3625 female  2007
 8        4675 male    2007
 9        3475 <NA>    2007
10        4250 <NA>    2007
# ℹ 334 more rows


I often use select() to make my data transformations more exclusive in a sub-set. I will ask you to select variables when preparing for statistical analyses, to prevent influence or leverage from unwanted values in your analyses.
Additional helper functions that come along with select() include:

starts_with("abc") would match names that begin with “abc”.
ends_with("xyz") would matches names that end with “xyz”.
contains("ijk") would match names that contain “ijk”.







Question 7
7a. Create a sub-set of the tidepool data that only includes the pool number, Shannon’s diversity metrics for macro sessile and mobile organisms, and the macro richness.
7b. Arrange your new subset in descending order by the pool number.






Remember earlier when I showed you how to rename your columns using base R? Well, like I said, you can also do that with dplyr using the command rename()!

rename(example_data, 
       old_col_name1 = new_col_name1, 
       old_col_name2 = new_col_name2)







Question 8
8a. Re-import the tidepool metadata set. What effect does this have to the work we’ve already done to the data set?
8b. Re-name the tidepool data frame object using snake_case once again (if you didn’t already do so during re-import). Using dplyr and the rename() command, rename all the columns in the tidepool metadata using snake_case.






Adding new columns or variables that are functions of existing columns is exactly where mutate() comes in.

# create a smaller sub-set of data to work with
penguins_reduced <- select(penguins, species, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)

# mutate to add column that calculates bill volume (BTW this is not a real measurement metric that ornithologists use, it's just an example I'm using for this)
mutate(penguins_reduced,
       bill_volume = bill_length_mm * bill_depth_mm)

# A tibble: 344 × 4
   species bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm bill_volume
   <fct>            <dbl>         <dbl>       <dbl>
 1 Adelie            39.1          18.7        731.
 2 Adelie            39.5          17.4        687.
 3 Adelie            40.3          18          725.
 4 Adelie            NA            NA           NA 
 5 Adelie            36.7          19.3        708.
 6 Adelie            39.3          20.6        810.
 7 Adelie            38.9          17.8        692.
 8 Adelie            39.2          19.6        768.
 9 Adelie            34.1          18.1        617.
10 Adelie            42            20.2        848.
# ℹ 334 more rows


The * is the multiplication mathematical operation we use in R. Other mathematical operations you might encounter include:

- = subtraction
/ = division
+ = addition
sqrt() = square root
log() = log







Question 9
Using the tidepool metadata sub-set you created in question 7, add together the Shannon’s Diversity for both sessile and mobile macroscopic organisms. Name this new variable appropriately.






Retrieve mean, medians, standard deviations, standard errors, and more with summarize(). Run the code below.

summarize(penguins, mean_bill_length_mm = mean(bill_length_mm))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_bill_length_mm
                <dbl>
1                  NA


Ruh roh… Okay that went wrong fast.
Well, do you recall when I was talking about how missing values can muck up your code earlier in the tutorial? Well the penguins data frame includes a few rows that have NA observations. To run summaries that include rows with NAs, use na.rm = TRUE.

summarize(penguins, mean_bill_length_mm = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_bill_length_mm
                <dbl>
1                43.9


na.rm = TRUE tells R to remove any missing values prior to computation. You could also leverage filter() to simply exclude any rows with NA observations prior to computation.

penguins_no_na <- filter(penguins, !is.na(bill_length_mm))

summarize(penguins_no_na, mean_bill_length_mm = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_bill_length_mm
                <dbl>
1                43.9


summarize() can do quick, general calculations for you, or can be used in tandem with group_by() to get grouped summary stats.

penguins %>% 
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(species_bill_means = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species   species_bill_means
  <fct>                  <dbl>
1 Adelie                  38.8
2 Chinstrap               48.8
3 Gentoo                  47.5


Okay, now if you’ve never seen a pipe before (or have no idea what a pipe even is (and no I don’t mean like the plumbing type of pipe)), this might look like complete and total gibberish. However, I think this is the most valuable skill I want you to take away from this tutorial: stringing together commands with pipes %>%.



Imagine that I’d like to get the mean of bill length and bill depth by species on each island from 2007 and 2008. You could do this step-by-step by:

selecting variables of interest
filtering out observations from 2009
grouping by species and island
summarizing your variables

Doing it like this is why people might come to hate coding, because this would be time intensive and yield a higher risk of making an up-stream mistake that you don’t catch until the very end of all your code. However, what if I told you all of this can be done in one consecutive “line” of code?
Take a look at the following:

penguins_bills <- penguins %>%
  select(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm) %>%
  filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm)) %>%
  group_by(species, island) %>%
  summarize(mean_length = mean(bill_length_mm),
            mean_depth = mean(bill_depth_mm))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'species'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


You should read this code as a series of interpretive statements: using the penguins data set (penguins_bills <- penguins %>%), select the variables (select(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)) then (%>%) filter out the NAs in bill_length_mm (filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm))) then (%>%) group by the categories of interest (group_by(species, island)) then (%>%)summarize. Every pipe (%>%) reads as “then”—the next step in your sequence of code.
A pipe (%>%) follows every primary command you’re asking R to compute, stringing them all together as one whole operation and saving it in a new data frame. You use pipes sequentially (left to right, top to bottom) which means you can un-write or over-write previous work in the same pipe string. Don’t do this. This is bad code practice. I will be sad if I see this in your code.






Question 10
Only using pipes:
10a. Using the tidepool metadata, group by tide pool size and get the mean Shannon’s diversity of both the mobile and sessile macro-organisms. Save this in a new data frame object (a tibble) and name it appropriately.
10b. Using the penguins data, create a new sub-set that only includes Chinstrap penguins and their bill measurements. Get the mean of the bill measurements in each year the penguins were measured.



Working with the pipe is one of the key features of the tidyverse that make it so powerful. The only exception to this, however, is ggplot2, which was written before the pipe was discovered. However, you can still string together piped, dplyr operations when plotting in ggplot2. I’ll show you how to do that next as we learn…




ggplot2 is the graphical system I will expect use to use when generating graphs and visualizations in this class. It’s a powerful tool because of it’s ability to be interfaced with dplyr and custom add-on packages for aesthetic purposes. We’ve already loaded the tidyverse package, which includes ggplot2, but it’s best practice to also load-in sub-packages on their own as well.
Add the following to your setup chunk:

library(ggplot2)

Let’s use the first graph we create to answer a hypothetical question we might have about the penguins data: do penguins with larger bills have a higher body mass?
Intuitively you might think that penguin bill size would scale proportionally with penguin body mass, but there’s no real way to tell without first visualizing it. We will use ggplot2 to do so.


To answer the above question, lets put bill length on the y-axis and body mass on the x-axis.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_point()








This plot shows what we might expect: as bill length increases, so too does body mass. What we’ve done here is used the function ggplot() to coordinate a system that you can add layers to. You add layers after each previous function using the +.
The first argument of ggplot() is the data = argument, which tells ggplot which data set you’re going to be visualizing. If you don’t specify this, ggplot will not know what to map your visualizations with (AKA: nothing will happen). Typing just ggplot(data = penguins) has a similar effect.

ggplot(data = penguins)








This is just an empty graph. R has no idea what you want you x- or y-axis to be. So you use the aes() call in ggplot() to specify what you’d like your x- or y-axis to be.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm))








Great, now R knows your graphing the penguins data set and visualizing bill_length_mm and body_mass_g on the y- and x-axis, respectively. However, we still need to tell R what type of graph this is.
There are a LOT of ways to visualize data, so making the choice about what type of graph to use can be one of the harder things you might have to do when your own data analysis rolls around. Here we’re using a scatter plot, which is specified by the geom_point argument.






To see examples of plots and what types of geom_ functions are at your disposal, you can head here.
This resource is genuinely incredible and will really help you expand your ggplot knowledge beyond just what I show you here. Please use it to your advantage!



Some geom_ calls we might use on our class include:

geom_point - makes a scatter plot
geom_bar - makes a bar plot

I will show you more functions of a bar plot later on, but understand that this is not the same thing as a column plot or a histogram.

geom_line - makes a line plot.

Can be combined with geom_point to make a line plot with points connecting the lines.

geom_boxplot - makes a box plot (one of my personal favorite plots)!
geom_violin - makes a violin plot (a better distribution visualizer than a box plot, and still just as cool when customized).
geom_histogram - makes a histogram
geom_qq - makes a qq plot.

This is very useful for data diagnosis and visualizing residuals.








Question 11
11a. Create a scatter plot using the tidepool metadata exploring the macro richness and tidepool volume.
11b. Create a bar plot showing the macro richness by tidepool size. (HINT: you will need to use the argument stat = ‘identity’ in your geom_ call).
11c. Use the ?geom_bar help command to pull up the help page for the geom_bar() command. Spend a little bit of time reading. Why do we need to use the stat = ‘identity’ argument when running the geom_bar()?
11d. What happens if we don’t add stat = ‘identity’?






How we choose to visualize data says almost just as much about what we’re saying in our results and our research. Making intentional, smart data visualization choices should be at the foremost of your work as you create your tidepool and culture experiment figures.
For example, the graph we created above with the penguins data set shows a (relatively) positive, linear relationship between bill length and body mass. However, ecological data is not as simple as: get bigger, grow bill.
What about species differences between bill length and body mass? Geographical differences? Have evolutionary pressures changes how bill size scales with body mass? Does sex play a role in this? Does island habitat influence these factors?
These are all questions that can be answered by mapping more aesthetics to your plot to convey complex information about your data. Two of the primary ways of doing this is with color = and shape = .

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point()








Whoa! We get very clear separation here between the three penguin species. Not every species shows that same positive linear relationship between bill length and body mass.
What about sex differences between and within the species?

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point()








If you spend some time parsing apart the circles and triangles, you can see that male penguins are more likely to have larger bills and a higher body mass when compared to females of the same species. You can answer these big questions by, again, making intentional choices with your graphs. By adding aesthetics to our graphs, we add depth and complexity to the data we’re visualizing.






Question 12
Using the tidepool metadata, create a scatter plot that shows the relationship between the sessile and mobile macro-organisms Shannon diversity colored by tidepool size.






What if I wanted to look at the species closer, without combining them together on one large graph? You’d use facet_wrap() to facet your plot by a single variable.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ species)








This applies the same variable mapping to each species, but graphs them separately according to whatever argument you use in facet_wrap(). We could also do the same thing with sex.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_point() +  
  facet_wrap(~ sex)








We can also facet by multiple variables.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) + 
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(species ~ sex, nrow = 2)














Question 13
Using the penguins data set, create a scatter plot faceted by species and island that explores the relationship between bill depth and bill length. Add additional aesthetic mappings as wanted or needed.






If you didn’t notice the NAs before when we colored by species and shaped our points by sex, then you for sure notice them now in the above graph. How might we get rid of those before we plot?
There are a few ways we could go about this. If we’re interested in sex-specific questions, and we have multiple observations that don’t include sex data, it might be prudent (for future analysis as well) to remove those NAs. To do this, we would use the drop_na() command as a part of a pipe string.
Remember when I said earlier we can also combine dplyr actions in one pipe with ggplot? To filter before you plot, you can use pipes to remove and/or filter unwanted observations in the same line of code as your ggplot command.

penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ sex)








Without saving this command in a data frame object, it does not permanently remove any of those rows with NAs, and only does so for this particular graph. This is a powerful tool that allows you to further focus your visualization to only the data you care about.






Question 14
Using the penguins data set and a pipe string, create a scatter plot faceted by species and sex that explores the relationship between bill depth and body mass only in the year 2007.






If I actually wanted to describe any linear (or non-linear) relationships in a graph, it will do me no good to just point at a scatter plot and claim there’s a linear relationship without a best-fit line there. You would usually back up a statement like this using a linear regression, but I won’t cover how to do those until a later tutorial.
The geom commands geom_smooth and geom_abline can be used in conjunction with geom commands like geom_point() to display a linear, quadratic, or non-linear relationship. For example: using geom_smooth() with a linear model (“lm”) displays a line according to a statistical method or pattern.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")








This will display a smoothed conditional mean line using the linear regression formula of y ~ x. This is essentially saying “the predicted response of body mass (y) equals the y-intercept plus the slope times the predictor of bill length (x), fitting a continuously smooth, straight line along those values”.
They grey shading along that line is the 95% confidence interval (or standard error) that the estimated line “fits” the data. We can remove the standard error shading by specifying se = FALSE.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)








The geom_abline command(s) literally fit a line to a specific point on your graph. Sub-commands in this include geom_vline for vertical lines and geom_hline for horizontal lines.
This would be great if, say for example, we’d like to point out a certain measurement that indicates growth or maturity in penguins (I’m not a penguin expert sadly, so don’t come for me if this isn’t accurate).

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 40, color = "blue", linewidth = 2, linetype = "dashed")








Within geom_ commands like this one, we can create specific aesthetic changes like color, linewidth, or linetype (all of which I’ve done above). We could also do the same for our linear regression line.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", linewidth = 2, linetype = "dashed")








We can also use geom_smooth in tandem with facets, to get a better idea of the fitted lines between categories of observations.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ species)








Each species have varying levels of strength to the slope of their linear lines. This tells a more nuanced story than simply “there is a positive linear relationship between body mass and bill length”. Using data visualization as leverage to tell that nuanced story will aid in your final speed talk and paper for this class.
We can also use geom_smooth alone to show general trends in numerical data.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_smooth()








This looks fairly relational to our scatter plot, but instead of a perfectly linear line, we’ve fit a best smoothed estimate line to every observed point. We can also add multiple smooths for multiple categories.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_smooth(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, 
                  linetype = species, color = species))








We now a smoothed picture of our body mass and bill length question. Take note of the standard errors of each smooth and how they differ. What might this tell you about the data?






Question 15
Using the tidepool metadata, explore the relationship between macro organism richness and tide pool volume between the two tide pool sizes (large and small). Fit a linear regression line to visualize the linear relationship between y ~ x.
(HINT: This one is tricky and will involve you using the mathematical operator log() on one of the axis. You can do so without piping using dplyr commands OR altering the data frame itself).






There are a plethora of add-ons and complexities that make ggplot simultaneously awesome and super confusing. I’ll try to run through a few need-to-know cases that might help serve you in this course.

Creating a bar chart: helps you get a better idea of the number of observations in a variable of interest (without the stat = 'identity' this time).


ggplot(data = penguins) + # there are varying numbers of observations between the species
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               color = species)) # outline color by species







# OR
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = species)) # fill bar color by species(much nicer, visually)







# OR
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = island)) # fill color by different variable than what you're plotting









Creating a bar chart with a proportion: might be a better way to view the counts of a variable, or understand how your data is structured (and because absolute counts can LIE)


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, y = ..prop.., group = 1))









Statistical transformation: using stat_summary is another useful way to get a fuller picture of your data.


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  stat_summary(aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm), 
               fun.min = min,
               fun.max = max,
               fun.y = median)









Position adjustments in bar charts.


# position = "fill"
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = island),
           position = "fill") # automatic proportion, stacked bar chart







# position = "dodge"
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = island),
           position = "dodge") # place values to the sides of one another









Manually creating your own discrete colors or shape scale using scale_shape_manual or scale_color_manual.


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8))








Shapes available to you in R include:

Colors available to you in R include (but are absolutely not limited to):


Adding axis and graph labels.


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex")









Changing the plot theme.


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex") +
  theme_bw()








There a lot of different themes includes with ggplot to make your plots more visually appealing. You can also install the add-on package ggthemes() to broaden your range of plot themes. To explore all the native themes in ggplot, head here.

Change the limits of your x- or y-axis as needed with xlim() or ylim().


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  xlim(0,7000) +
  ylim(0,70) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex") +
  theme_bw()









Coordinate flip with coord_flip().


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  stat_summary(aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm), 
               fun.min = min,
               fun.max = max,
               fun.y = median) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)", 
       x = "Species") +
  theme_bw()









Saving your plot with ggsave().


plot_1 <- penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex") +
  theme_bw()

ggsave('figure_or_plot_name_here.png',                      # saved plot name
       path = "/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Plots",  # a path OR your wd
       width = 10,                                          # plot width
       length = 7,                                          # plot length
       units = c("in"),                                     # unit of width/length
       dpi = 300)                                           # plot resoltion

Understand variables, vectors, and logical operators.

title: Tutorial 1
text:
For this tutorial you are expected to already know how to create a new .rmd file, get your working directory, and set your working directory. If you are not able to do so, return to Tutorial 0 for a quick review on these skills.






Important



To start, ensure your R-Prep.rmd file is in your Coding Tutorials folder. If you do not have a coding tutorials folder, make a folder path on your computer like the following: /yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials.
Alongside answering assignment questions in the R-Prep assignment, I would also like you to create your own Rmarkdown file called tutorial-1.rmd to follow along as you work through this tutorial. Do not do work for your R Preparedness assignment in your tutorial-1.rmd file. Instead use this file as a way to test your skills and practice your code.
Write your final answers for the assignment is the R-Prep.rmd only. Do not submit the tutorial-1.rmd as the submission for your R Preparedness assignment.


In this tutorial we will be using the package palmerpenguins as well as an example data set provided on the class canvas page. Palmer penguins is a CRAN data set provided that is a better alternative to the iris data set. If you’d like to know more about why we won’t be using iris in this course, you can read more here.
Palmer penguins is a data set including size measurements, clutch observations, and blood isotope ratios for adult foraging Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo penguins observed on islands in the Palmer Archipelago near Palmer Station, Antarctica.
First, install palmerpenguins into your local environment by using the command install.packages().

install.packages("palmerpenguins")

When installing multiple packages at once, you will use something known as a character vector combine ( c() ) followed by the list of packages you’re installing.

install.packages(c("package1", "package2", "package3", "package4"))

The packages are now installed, but not currently active in our library. To make the packages usable and active in our current session, we use the library() command.

library(palmerpenguins)







Tip



To keep things neat and tidy, I suggest using one R chunk to complete your “setup” (i.e., package installation, library imports, and other global options you set in your script). It would look something like this in your .rmd file:

# gloabl chunk options (example)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

# getting and setting working directory. Always double check if this worked
getwd()
setwd("/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 1")
getwd()

# install packages 
install.packages(c("package1", 
                   "package2", 
                   "package3", 
                   "package4")
                 )

# load multiple packages at once
my_packages <- c("package1", # perhaps note what this package is used for
                   "package2", # bwt these are not real packages
                   "package3", # don't try to actually install them
                   "package4") # (HINT: it won't work)
lapply(my_packages, library, character.only = TRUE)



Try it yourself: Create a new chunk and name it “data import”. In this class, we will save our data sets as .csv files (comma separated files). As scientists, we may use Excel or Google Sheets to input data, but it is best practice to save individual data sets as .csv files for simple, easy importing. To read a .xls file into R, you would need to install a third party package which adds an additional computing step during set up.
Navigate to our class canvas page. Go to Files and open the Coding Tutorials folder. Download the metadata_tidepool.csv file and save it in your Coding Tutorials folder. If you’ve properly set your working directory and saved the file to the correct location, this next step will be easy.
Import your .csv data set using the read.csv() command and saving it in a data frame object called ‘data’.

data <- read.csv("metadata_tidepool.csv", header = TRUE)

The argument header = TRUE will ensure that when you import your .csv, R is treating the first row of your data as column names. You would set this as header = FALSE if your data set did not contain column names.
Great, now we have two different data sets to play around with. Let’s start with Palmer penguins.






Question 1
1a. Get and set your working directory based on the suggested file path in Tutorial 0.
1b. Install the Palmer penguins data set onto your computer.
1c. Import the tidepool metadata practice data set.

title: Tutorial 1
text:
It’s good practice to ensure that when you import a new data frame into your computing environment, you ensure everything looks approximately as it should (i.e., variable names are included and correct, variable types seem accurate to your data, and number of observations is approximately accurate).
This is simply done with three commands that I suggest you always run when first importing data into R: summary(), head(), and tail().

summary(penguins)

title: Tutorial 1
text:
Looking at the summary of penguins reveals this is an 8 variable data frame: 3 categorical variables (species, island, sex), 4 numeric variables (bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g), and 1 integer variable (year).
To check what kind of variable is contained within a data frame object, you use the class() command.

# syntax example of class()
class(dataframe$variableofinterest)

The command is broken down as class(x$variable) where x is the data frame object you’re inspecting, $ is the extraction operator of x, and variable is simply the name of the variable you’re extracting from x.

class(penguins$species)

[1] "factor"


This returns the class of the variable species extracted from the penguins data frame. You should see “factor” in your own .rmd, like above.
It’s important you understand and can differentiate between the atomic data types you might encounter when using R. However, for this class and likely beyond, you’ll only really need to focus on the first four.










Type
Description
Example Value
class() Output
Common Use Case




Numeric
Default for all real numbers; Can be continuous or discrete.
10 or 2.1415
“numeric”
Continuous measurements, math calculations


Integer
Whole numbers explicitly defined.
7L or year
“integer”
Discretely counted numbers, IDs, loops, dates


Character
Text strings wrapped in single or double quotes, categorical variables with no numerical value.
“hello” or “yes” or “female”
“factor”
Names, descriptive labels, text data


Logical
Boolean states representing truth values.
TRUE and/or FALSE
“logical”
Filtering data, evaluating conditions


Complex
Numbers containing real and imaginary components.
3 + 2i
“complex”
Purely mathematical or signal functions.


Raw
Unprocessessed binary data stored ad individual bytes.
as.raw(43)
“raw”
Reading binary files or protocol streams.



In R, we have values (the data), vectors (the container), and variables (the names).
Values in R must belong to an atomic data type (like the six types above).
Vectors are an ordered collection of values. Vectors are homogeneous, meaning all values inside a single vector must be of the same type.

To create a vector you combine values using the c() (combine) function.
If you mix data types, R forces lower types into higher types (Logical < Integer < Double < Character) without throwing an error.

For example, c(5, "Hello") forces the numeric 5 to become a factor "5".

Access specific elements of a vector by using square brackets []. R uses 1-based indexing, so the first element is at position 1.


# create a numeric vector
ages <- c(15, 20, 25, 30)

# extracting the second element follows 1-based idexing
ages[2] # should return 20

[1] 20


Variables are the “labeled boxes” of your data. When you write code, you use the assignment operator <- to bind a name (the variable) to a data object (like a vector).

While the equals sign = works for assignment, <- is preferred (and the widely recognized standard for assignment).
Variable names are case- and space-sensitive (my_data is different from My_Data or my_ data).
Assigning a new value to an existing variable overwrites its previous contents.


fruits <- c("apple", "cherry", "blueberry")
print(fruits)

[1] "apple"     "cherry"    "blueberry"



# assigning a new value will un-write the previously assigned content of "fruits"
fruits <- c("lettuce", "carrots", "celery") 
print(fruits)

[1] "lettuce" "carrots" "celery" 


We use the print() function to give the output or generate the contents of a vector or object into the R Console.
All R statements where you create objects , assignment statements, have the same form: object_name <- value. Again, don’t use =. It’s just confusing.






Tip



Getting tired of continuously, manually typing <- ? Well, you’re just in luck!! Statisticians and those who use R agree with that sentiment, so there’s actually a keyboard shortcut! Just type Alt + - and voila! You’re very own assignment argument.
This is also great because R automatically gives you spaces on both sides of the assignment arrow, which (again) is good coding practice and etiquette. Code is miserable to read on a good day, so giveyourselfabreakand use spaces.


This harkens to the code etiquette tab, but please ensure you’re giving your assigned objects, vectors, data frames (or literally everything else) a descriptive name. In this course, I’d like you to use snake_case, where you will separate lowercase words of a named object with _.

i_use_snake_case
otherPeopleUseCamelCase
some.people.use.periods
And_aFew.People_RENOUNCEconvention.like_fre_AKS

Sometimes the data you have doesn’t match your coding conventions. Our tide pool meta data mixes snake_case and camelCase. This is a very common issue and often happens with data sets that include multiple contributors.
It is best practice to agree and stick to a single convention when collaborating. To maintain continuity, you can rename variables using your agreed-upon convention.
For us, this convention is snake_case. To do this, you can use dplyr or base R. We’ll learn more about dplyr in a moment, but for now lets learn how to do this in base R.

# explicit name matching in base R to re-name data frame variables
names(data) [names(data) == "Old_Column_Name"] <- "new_column_name"

####OR####

# rename ALL columns all at once
names(data) <- c("column_1", "column_2", "column_3", "column_4")







Question 3
3a. What is the atomic data type of tide pool size? How many tide pool sizes are there?
3b. Rename the tidepool metadata object (if you haven’t already) using snake_case convention.
3c. Rename all variables in your new tide pool data frame to match snake_case conventions.

title: Tutorial 1
text:
If you’ve noticed the double semi-colon after certain names of things (e.g., knitr:: , ggplot:: , dplyr::) this is an easy indication I am talking about a package.
When installing packages that overwrite previously installed or loaded packages in R, but you still want to use those functions, you’ll need to specify what package relates to what function. The two most common conflicts are two functions from the stats:: package (which is pre-loaded in base R):

stats::filter()

stats::lag()

If you receive conflict messages when running filter() or lag(), it’s likely because you’ve overwritten one or the other with either stats:: or dplyr::. Use the package that correlates to the command you’re trying to execute by indicating so with ::.


To transform our data, we will be using the dplyr package, which is a small component of the tidyverse package suite. If you haven’t done so before, install and load the tidyverse and dplyr packages in your setup chunk.

install.packages(c("tidyverse", "dplyr"))

# load multiple packages at once
CRAN_packages <- c("tidyverse", "dplyr")
lapply(CRAN_packages, library, character.only = TRUE)

CRAN packages are add-on modules written for R by other programmers. lapply() applies your vector CRAN_packages using the library() function, ensuring R passes your string of variables (your packages) in your vector as actual package names R will recognize. This allows you to load multiple packages at once without having to retype library() over and over.






Question 4
Use code to install the tidyverse and dplyr packages; Load the packages into your working session.






There are five key functions that make dplyr extraordinarily valuable for data visualization and transformation.

Pick observations by their values using filter()
Reorder rows with arrange()
Pick variables by their names with select()
Create new variables with functions of existing variables using mutate()
Collapse many values down to a single summary with summarize()

These commands are often used in conjunction with group_by(), which changes the scope of each function by going group-by-group, if desired. All of these functions together provide the verbage of data manipulation. All of the verbs (functions) work similarly where:

Argument one is the data frame.
Subsequent arguments describe what you’d like to actually do with the data.
The result is saved in a new data frame.

Do these things and you can successfully manipulate any data frame.



filter() subsets observations based on their values followed by a logical operator that you provide. For example, say I want to only look at Chinstrap penguin measurements from 2007.

filter(penguins, species == "Chinstrap", year == 2007)

# A tibble: 26 × 8
   species   island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
 1 Chinstrap Dream            46.5          17.9               192        3500
 2 Chinstrap Dream            50            19.5               196        3900
 3 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          19.2               193        3650
 4 Chinstrap Dream            45.4          18.7               188        3525
 5 Chinstrap Dream            52.7          19.8               197        3725
 6 Chinstrap Dream            45.2          17.8               198        3950
 7 Chinstrap Dream            46.1          18.2               178        3250
 8 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          18.2               197        3750
 9 Chinstrap Dream            46            18.9               195        4150
10 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          19.9               198        3700
# ℹ 16 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>


dplyr functions never permanently modify their inputs, so if you want to save the result, you’ll need to use an assignment <- operator.

chinstrap_2007_df <- filter(penguins, species == "Chinstrap", year == 2007)

R will either print out the results if you explicitly ask using print(), or save them into a variable without printing. If you want to print and save it without writing an extra line of code, wrap the entire argument in parentheses.

(chinstrap_2007_df <- filter(penguins, species == "Chinstrap", year == 2007))

# A tibble: 26 × 8
   species   island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
 1 Chinstrap Dream            46.5          17.9               192        3500
 2 Chinstrap Dream            50            19.5               196        3900
 3 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          19.2               193        3650
 4 Chinstrap Dream            45.4          18.7               188        3525
 5 Chinstrap Dream            52.7          19.8               197        3725
 6 Chinstrap Dream            45.2          17.8               198        3950
 7 Chinstrap Dream            46.1          18.2               178        3250
 8 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          18.2               197        3750
 9 Chinstrap Dream            46            18.9               195        4150
10 Chinstrap Dream            51.3          19.9               198        3700
# ℹ 16 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>


To filter effectively, you need to understand how to correctly select comparison and logical operators. R gives you quite a few:

>, greater than
>=, greater than or equal to
<, less than
<=, less than or equal to
!=, not equal to
==, equal to

Boolean operators combine comparisons and logical operators when you’d like to filter multiple things at once. This is where filtering is the most confusing; Even after 4 years of using dplyr consistently, I am still struggling to understand this. So, I am under no expectation that you are to master this, I am simply giving you this information, and you may do with it what you’d like.
Boolean Operations are as follows:

& is “and”
| is “or”
! is “not”


Say, for example, I’d like to find penguins that were measured in 2007 or 2008.

filter(penguins, year == 2007 | year == 2008)

This reads in plain English as “find all penguins measured in year 2007 or year 2008”. You can also simplify if you’re filtering the data frame by the same variable by using x %in% y.

filter(penguins, year %in% c(2007,2008))

Or for character variables…

filter(penguins, species %in% c("Chinstrap", "Adelie"))

What if I’d like to do more complicated sub-setting? For example, I’d like to filter for every penguin that isn’t a Adelie and Chinstrap penguin and that have bills <40mm.

filter(penguins, !(species %in% c("Adelie", "Chinstrap") & bill_length_mm > 40))

When filtering, you can simplify by remembering that:

!(x & y) is the same as !x & !y
!(x | y) is the same as !x & !y




Filtering and using comparison operators is often tricky when values are missing. NA represents an unknown value. Unknown values are contagious, and any operation or command that requires all values won’t run when values are missing. For example:

# let x be Penelope's age. Sadly, we have no idea how old Penelope is.
x <- NA

# let y be Jack's age. We also don't know how old Jack is. Bummer.
y <- NA

# Are Jack and Penelope the same age?
x == y

[1] NA

# We don't know because Jack and Penelope's ages are NA's!!!!!

If you’d like to know if a value is missing, use is.na().

is.na(x)

[1] TRUE


Filtering data will only include rows where the condition of is.na() == FALSE.

fake_data <- tibble(x = c(1, NA, 3))
filter(fake_data, x > 1)

# A tibble: 1 × 1
      x
  <dbl>
1     3


But perhaps we’d like to include all rows, even if they have NAs, then you’d explicitly ask R to do so.

filter(fake_data, is.na(x) | x > 1)

# A tibble: 2 × 1
      x
  <dbl>
1    NA
2     3








Question 5
5a. Filter the tide pool data to only include large tide pools with a macro_richness that is >= to 10.
5b. Filter the Palmer penguins data set to only include male penguins that were measured in every year except 2007 on Biscoe island (HINT: try using the ! logical operator to exclude 2007).






arrange() changes the orders of rows, which can be useful when plotting or doing ranked statistical analyses.

arrange(penguins, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)

   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>

 2 Adelie  Dream               33.1          16.1               178        2900

10 Adelie  Biscoe              35            17.9               190        3450

# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>


arrange() automatically sorts the values of bill_length_mm and bill_depth_mm in ascending order. If I wanted these variables to be in descending order, I’d specify with desc().

arrange(penguins, desc(bill_length_mm), desc(bill_depth_mm))

# A tibble: 344 × 8
   species   island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
 1 Gentoo    Biscoe           59.6          17                 230        6050
 2 Chinstrap Dream            58            17.8               181        3700
 3 Gentoo    Biscoe           55.9          17                 228        5600
 4 Chinstrap Dream            55.8          19.8               207        4000

10 Chinstrap Dream            52.8          20                 205        4550
# ℹ 334 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>


If you provide more than one column when arranging, each additional column will be used to break ties in the values of preceding columns. Above, we’ve arranged penguin observations by largest bill length, further sorted by largest bill depth. See what happens to the first few rows when we instead sort by bill_depth_mm first.

arrange(penguins, desc(bill_depth_mm), desc(bill_length_mm))

# A tibble: 344 × 8
   species   island   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>     <fct>             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>

Missing values are always sorted at the end:

Question 6
6a. How could you use arrange() to sort all missing values to the start? (HINT: try using is.na()).
6b. In the tide pool data frame, arrange our observations in descending order by the Macro-organism Shannon mean and the Macro-organism richness.






Its not uncommon to receive data sets with hundreds or maybe even thousands of variables. Working with a data frame that dense is computationally expensive, so sub-setting down to the variables of interest is best practice when dealing with data like that. select() gives you the power to accomplish that.

# select columns of interest
select(penguins, species, island, sex, year)

# A tibble: 344 × 4
   species island    sex     year
   <fct>   <fct>     <fct>  <int>

10 Adelie  Torgersen <NA>    2007

select(penguins, species:flipper_length_mm)

I often use select() to make my data transformations more exclusive in a sub-set. I will ask you to select variables when preparing for statistical analyses, to prevent influence or leverage from unwanted values in your analyses.
Additional helper functions that come along with select() include:

starts_with("abc") would match names that begin with “abc”.
ends_with("xyz") would matches names that end with “xyz”.
contains("ijk") would match names that contain “ijk”.







Question 7
7a. Create a sub-set of the tidepool data that only includes the pool number, Shannon’s diversity metrics for macro sessile and mobile organisms, and the macro richness.
7b. Arrange your new subset in descending order by the pool number.






Remember earlier when I showed you how to rename your columns using base R? Well, like I said, you can also do that with dplyr using the command rename()!

rename(example_data, 
       old_col_name1 = new_col_name1, 
       old_col_name2 = new_col_name2)







Question 8
8a. Re-import the tidepool metadata set. What effect does this have to the work we’ve already done to the data set?
8b. Re-name the tidepool data frame object using snake_case once again (if you didn’t already do so during re-import). Using dplyr and the rename() command, rename all the columns in the tidepool metadata using snake_case.






Adding new columns or variables that are functions of existing columns is exactly where mutate() comes in.

# create a smaller sub-set of data to work with
penguins_reduced <- select(penguins, species, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)

# mutate to add column that calculates bill volume (BTW this is not a real measurement metric that ornithologists use, it's just an example I'm using for this)
mutate(penguins_reduced,
       bill_volume = bill_length_mm * bill_depth_mm)

   species bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm bill_volume

10 Adelie            42            20.2        848.
