The * is the multiplication mathematical operation we use in R. Other mathematical operations you might encounter include:

- = subtraction
/ = division
+ = addition
sqrt() = square root
log() = log







Question 9
Using the tidepool metadata sub-set you created in question 7, add together the Shannon’s Diversity for both sessile and mobile macroscopic organisms. Name this new variable appropriately.






Retrieve mean, medians, standard deviations, standard errors, and more with summarize(). Run the code below.

summarize(penguins, mean_bill_length_mm = mean(bill_length_mm))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_bill_length_mm
                <dbl>
1                  NA


Ruh roh… Okay that went wrong fast.
Well, do you recall when I was talking about how missing values can muck up your code earlier in the tutorial? Well the penguins data frame includes a few rows that have NA observations. To run summaries that include rows with NAs, use na.rm = TRUE.

summarize(penguins, mean_bill_length_mm = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_bill_length_mm
                <dbl>
1                43.9


na.rm = TRUE tells R to remove any missing values prior to computation. You could also leverage filter() to simply exclude any rows with NA observations prior to computation.

penguins_no_na <- filter(penguins, !is.na(bill_length_mm))

summarize(penguins_no_na, mean_bill_length_mm = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_bill_length_mm
                <dbl>
1                43.9


summarize() can do quick, general calculations for you, or can be used in tandem with group_by() to get grouped summary stats.

penguins %>% 
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(species_bill_means = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species   species_bill_means
  <fct>                  <dbl>
1 Adelie                  38.8
2 Chinstrap               48.8
3 Gentoo                  47.5


Okay, now if you’ve never seen a pipe before (or have no idea what a pipe even is (and no I don’t mean like the plumbing type of pipe)), this might look like complete and total gibberish. However, I think this is the most valuable skill I want you to take away from this tutorial: stringing together commands with pipes %>%.



Imagine that I’d like to get the mean of bill length and bill depth by species on each island from 2007 and 2008. You could do this step-by-step by:

selecting variables of interest
filtering out observations from 2009
grouping by species and island
summarizing your variables

Doing it like this is why people might come to hate coding, because this would be time intensive and yield a higher risk of making an up-stream mistake that you don’t catch until the very end of all your code. However, what if I told you all of this can be done in one consecutive “line” of code?
Take a look at the following:

penguins_bills <- penguins %>%
  select(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm) %>%
  filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm)) %>%
  group_by(species, island) %>%
  summarize(mean_length = mean(bill_length_mm),
            mean_depth = mean(bill_depth_mm))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'species'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


You should read this code as a series of interpretive statements: using the penguins data set (penguins_bills <- penguins %>%), select the variables (select(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)) then (%>%) filter out the NAs in bill_length_mm (filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm))) then (%>%) group by the categories of interest (group_by(species, island)) then (%>%)summarize. Every pipe (%>%) reads as “then”—the next step in your sequence of code.
A pipe (%>%) follows every primary command you’re asking R to compute, stringing them all together as one whole operation and saving it in a new data frame. You use pipes sequentially (left to right, top to bottom) which means you can un-write or over-write previous work in the same pipe string. Don’t do this. This is bad code practice. I will be sad if I see this in your code.






Question 10
Only using pipes:
10a. Using the tidepool metadata, group by tide pool size and get the mean Shannon’s diversity of both the mobile and sessile macro-organisms. Save this in a new data frame object (a tibble) and name it appropriately.
10b. Using the penguins data, create a new sub-set that only includes Chinstrap penguins and their bill measurements. Get the mean of the bill measurements in each year the penguins were measured.



Working with the pipe is one of the key features of the tidyverse that make it so powerful. The only exception to this, however, is ggplot2, which was written before the pipe was discovered. However, you can still string together piped, dplyr operations when plotting in ggplot2. I’ll show you how to do that next as we learn…

title: Tutorial 1
text:
ggplot2 is the graphical system I will expect use to use when generating graphs and visualizations in this class. It’s a powerful tool because of it’s ability to be interfaced with dplyr and custom add-on packages for aesthetic purposes. We’ve already loaded the tidyverse package, which includes ggplot2, but it’s best practice to also load-in sub-packages on their own as well.
Add the following to your setup chunk:

library(ggplot2)

Let’s use the first graph we create to answer a hypothetical question we might have about the penguins data: do penguins with larger bills have a higher body mass?
Intuitively you might think that penguin bill size would scale proportionally with penguin body mass, but there’s no real way to tell without first visualizing it. We will use ggplot2 to do so.


To answer the above question, lets put bill length on the y-axis and body mass on the x-axis.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_point()








This plot shows what we might expect: as bill length increases, so too does body mass. What we’ve done here is used the function ggplot() to coordinate a system that you can add layers to. You add layers after each previous function using the +.
The first argument of ggplot() is the data = argument, which tells ggplot which data set you’re going to be visualizing. If you don’t specify this, ggplot will not know what to map your visualizations with (AKA: nothing will happen). Typing just ggplot(data = penguins) has a similar effect.

ggplot(data = penguins)








This is just an empty graph. R has no idea what you want you x- or y-axis to be. So you use the aes() call in ggplot() to specify what you’d like your x- or y-axis to be.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm))








Great, now R knows your graphing the penguins data set and visualizing bill_length_mm and body_mass_g on the y- and x-axis, respectively. However, we still need to tell R what type of graph this is.
There are a LOT of ways to visualize data, so making the choice about what type of graph to use can be one of the harder things you might have to do when your own data analysis rolls around. Here we’re using a scatter plot, which is specified by the geom_point argument.






To see examples of plots and what types of geom_ functions are at your disposal, you can head here.
This resource is genuinely incredible and will really help you expand your ggplot knowledge beyond just what I show you here. Please use it to your advantage!



Some geom_ calls we might use on our class include:

geom_point - makes a scatter plot
geom_bar - makes a bar plot

I will show you more functions of a bar plot later on, but understand that this is not the same thing as a column plot or a histogram.

geom_line - makes a line plot.

Can be combined with geom_point to make a line plot with points connecting the lines.

geom_boxplot - makes a box plot (one of my personal favorite plots)!
geom_violin - makes a violin plot (a better distribution visualizer than a box plot, and still just as cool when customized).
geom_histogram - makes a histogram
geom_qq - makes a qq plot.

This is very useful for data diagnosis and visualizing residuals.








Question 11
11a. Create a scatter plot using the tidepool metadata exploring the macro richness and tidepool volume.
11b. Create a bar plot showing the macro richness by tidepool size. (HINT: you will need to use the argument stat = ‘identity’ in your geom_ call).
11c. Use the ?geom_bar help command to pull up the help page for the geom_bar() command. Spend a little bit of time reading. Why do we need to use the stat = ‘identity’ argument when running the geom_bar()?
11d. What happens if we don’t add stat = ‘identity’?






How we choose to visualize data says almost just as much about what we’re saying in our results and our research. Making intentional, smart data visualization choices should be at the foremost of your work as you create your tidepool and culture experiment figures.
For example, the graph we created above with the penguins data set shows a (relatively) positive, linear relationship between bill length and body mass. However, ecological data is not as simple as: get bigger, grow bill.
What about species differences between bill length and body mass? Geographical differences? Have evolutionary pressures changes how bill size scales with body mass? Does sex play a role in this? Does island habitat influence these factors?
These are all questions that can be answered by mapping more aesthetics to your plot to convey complex information about your data. Two of the primary ways of doing this is with color = and shape = .

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point()








Whoa! We get very clear separation here between the three penguin species. Not every species shows that same positive linear relationship between bill length and body mass.
What about sex differences between and within the species?

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point()








If you spend some time parsing apart the circles and triangles, you can see that male penguins are more likely to have larger bills and a higher body mass when compared to females of the same species. You can answer these big questions by, again, making intentional choices with your graphs. By adding aesthetics to our graphs, we add depth and complexity to the data we’re visualizing.






Question 12
Using the tidepool metadata, create a scatter plot that shows the relationship between the sessile and mobile macro-organisms Shannon diversity colored by tidepool size.






What if I wanted to look at the species closer, without combining them together on one large graph? You’d use facet_wrap() to facet your plot by a single variable.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ species)








This applies the same variable mapping to each species, but graphs them separately according to whatever argument you use in facet_wrap(). We could also do the same thing with sex.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_point() +  
  facet_wrap(~ sex)








We can also facet by multiple variables.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) + 
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(species ~ sex, nrow = 2)














Question 13
Using the penguins data set, create a scatter plot faceted by species and island that explores the relationship between bill depth and bill length. Add additional aesthetic mappings as wanted or needed.






If you didn’t notice the NAs before when we colored by species and shaped our points by sex, then you for sure notice them now in the above graph. How might we get rid of those before we plot?
There are a few ways we could go about this. If we’re interested in sex-specific questions, and we have multiple observations that don’t include sex data, it might be prudent (for future analysis as well) to remove those NAs. To do this, we would use the drop_na() command as a part of a pipe string.
Remember when I said earlier we can also combine dplyr actions in one pipe with ggplot? To filter before you plot, you can use pipes to remove and/or filter unwanted observations in the same line of code as your ggplot command.

penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ sex)








Without saving this command in a data frame object, it does not permanently remove any of those rows with NAs, and only does so for this particular graph. This is a powerful tool that allows you to further focus your visualization to only the data you care about.






Question 14
Using the penguins data set and a pipe string, create a scatter plot faceted by species and sex that explores the relationship between bill depth and body mass only in the year 2007.






If I actually wanted to describe any linear (or non-linear) relationships in a graph, it will do me no good to just point at a scatter plot and claim there’s a linear relationship without a best-fit line there. You would usually back up a statement like this using a linear regression, but I won’t cover how to do those until a later tutorial.
The geom commands geom_smooth and geom_abline can be used in conjunction with geom commands like geom_point() to display a linear, quadratic, or non-linear relationship. For example: using geom_smooth() with a linear model (“lm”) displays a line according to a statistical method or pattern.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")








This will display a smoothed conditional mean line using the linear regression formula of y ~ x. This is essentially saying “the predicted response of body mass (y) equals the y-intercept plus the slope times the predictor of bill length (x), fitting a continuously smooth, straight line along those values”.
They grey shading along that line is the 95% confidence interval (or standard error) that the estimated line “fits” the data. We can remove the standard error shading by specifying se = FALSE.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)








The geom_abline command(s) literally fit a line to a specific point on your graph. Sub-commands in this include geom_vline for vertical lines and geom_hline for horizontal lines.
This would be great if, say for example, we’d like to point out a certain measurement that indicates growth or maturity in penguins (I’m not a penguin expert sadly, so don’t come for me if this isn’t accurate).

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 40, color = "blue", linewidth = 2, linetype = "dashed")








Within geom_ commands like this one, we can create specific aesthetic changes like color, linewidth, or linetype (all of which I’ve done above). We could also do the same for our linear regression line.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", linewidth = 2, linetype = "dashed")








We can also use geom_smooth in tandem with facets, to get a better idea of the fitted lines between categories of observations.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ species)








Each species have varying levels of strength to the slope of their linear lines. This tells a more nuanced story than simply “there is a positive linear relationship between body mass and bill length”. Using data visualization as leverage to tell that nuanced story will aid in your final speed talk and paper for this class.
We can also use geom_smooth alone to show general trends in numerical data.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  geom_smooth()








This looks fairly relational to our scatter plot, but instead of a perfectly linear line, we’ve fit a best smoothed estimate line to every observed point. We can also add multiple smooths for multiple categories.

ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_smooth(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, 
                  linetype = species, color = species))








We now a smoothed picture of our body mass and bill length question. Take note of the standard errors of each smooth and how they differ. What might this tell you about the data?






Question 15
Using the tidepool metadata, explore the relationship between macro organism richness and tide pool volume between the two tide pool sizes (large and small). Fit a linear regression line to visualize the linear relationship between y ~ x.
(HINT: This one is tricky and will involve you using the mathematical operator log() on one of the axis. You can do so without piping using dplyr commands OR altering the data frame itself).






There are a plethora of add-ons and complexities that make ggplot simultaneously awesome and super confusing. I’ll try to run through a few need-to-know cases that might help serve you in this course.

Creating a bar chart: helps you get a better idea of the number of observations in a variable of interest (without the stat = 'identity' this time).


ggplot(data = penguins) + # there are varying numbers of observations between the species
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               color = species)) # outline color by species







# OR
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = species)) # fill bar color by species(much nicer, visually)







# OR
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = island)) # fill color by different variable than what you're plotting









Creating a bar chart with a proportion: might be a better way to view the counts of a variable, or understand how your data is structured (and because absolute counts can LIE)


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, y = ..prop.., group = 1))









Statistical transformation: using stat_summary is another useful way to get a fuller picture of your data.


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  stat_summary(aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm), 
               fun.min = min,
               fun.max = max,
               fun.y = median)









Position adjustments in bar charts.


# position = "fill"
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = island),
           position = "fill") # automatic proportion, stacked bar chart







# position = "dodge"
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = species, 
               fill = island),
           position = "dodge") # place values to the sides of one another









Manually creating your own discrete colors or shape scale using scale_shape_manual or scale_color_manual.


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8))








Shapes available to you in R include:

Colors available to you in R include (but are absolutely not limited to):


Adding axis and graph labels.


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex")









Changing the plot theme.


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex") +
  theme_bw()








There a lot of different themes includes with ggplot to make your plots more visually appealing. You can also install the add-on package ggthemes() to broaden your range of plot themes. To explore all the native themes in ggplot, head here.

Change the limits of your x- or y-axis as needed with xlim() or ylim().


penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  xlim(0,7000) +
  ylim(0,70) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex") +
  theme_bw()









Coordinate flip with coord_flip().


ggplot(data = penguins) +
  stat_summary(aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm), 
               fun.min = min,
               fun.max = max,
               fun.y = median) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)", 
       x = "Species") +
  theme_bw()









Saving your plot with ggsave().


plot_1 <- penguins %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm, color = species, shape = sex)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("orange", "blue", "purple")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(19, 8)) +
  labs(y = "Bill Length (mm)",
       x = "Body Mass (g)",
       title = "Plot 1",
       subtitle = "Penguin bill length and body mass by species and sex",
       color = "Species",
       shape = "Sex") +
  theme_bw()

ggsave('figure_or_plot_name_here.png',                      # saved plot name
       path = "/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Plots",  # a path OR your wd
       width = 10,                                          # plot width
       length = 7,                                          # plot length
       units = c("in"),                                     # unit of width/length
       dpi = 300)                                           # plot resoltion

title: Code Etiquette 101
text:
Now that we’ve actually started writing and running our own commands, now is the best time to introduce you to proper code etiquette. We might also refer to this as “coding style” which actually matters a lot more than you might think.
Good coding style is like using correct punctuation or capitalization; You can manage without it, but it sure makes things easier to read. As with styles of punctuation, there are many possible variations that one can use, but over the years language conventions have settled on “correct” ways of using punctuation. The same thing has happened with code. There is an “unspoken” grammar that is used when coding that has nothing to do with the actual code.


Good style is important because while your code often only has one author, it’ll usually have multiple readers (like me!!!!). This is especially true when you’re writing code with others. Turning in code that is pleasant to read, easy to follow, and logical makes the grading process way easier (and, once again, aids in reproducibility)! We can workshop how your code should look in office hours, if needed.

title: Code Etiquette 101
text: Good style is important because while your code often only has one author, it’ll usually have multiple readers (like me!!!!). This is especially true when you’re writing code with others. Turning in code that is pleasant to read, easy to follow, and logical makes the grading process way easier (and, once again, aids in reproducibility)! title: Code Etiquette 101
text:
File Names
File names should be meaningful and end in .R.

# Good
fit-models.R
utility-functions.R

# Bad
foo.r
stuff.r

If files need to be run in sequence, prefix them with numbers:

0-download.R
1-parse.R
2-explore.R

title: Code Etiquette 101
text:
Object and Vector Names

“There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.”
— Phil Karlton

Variable and function names should be lowercase. Use an underscore (_) to separate words within a name. Generally, variable names should be nouns and function names should be verbs. Strive for names that are concise and meaningful (this is not easy!!!!).

# Good
day_one
day_1

# Bad
first_day_of_the_month
DayOne
dayone
djm1

Where possible, avoid using names of existing functions and variables. Doing so will cause confusion for the readers of your code.

# Bad
T <- FALSE
c <- 10
mean <- function(x) sum(x)

title: Code Etiquette 101
text:
Spacing
Place spaces around all infix operators (=, +, -, <-, etc.). The same rule applies when using = in function calls. Always put a space after a comma, and never before (just like in regular English).

# Good
average <- mean(feet / 12 + inches, na.rm = TRUE)

# Bad
average<-mean(feet/12+inches,na.rm=TRUE)

BUT, There’s a small exception to this rule: :, :: and ::: don’t need spaces around them.

# Good
x <- 1:10
base::get

# Bad
x <- 1 : 10
base :: get

Place a space before left parentheses, except in a function call.

# Good
if (debug) do(x)
plot(x, y)

# Bad
if(debug)do(x)
plot (x, y)

Extra spacing (i.e., more than one space in a row) is okay if it improves alignment of equal signs or assignments (<-).

list(
  total =  a + b + c, 
  mean  = (a + b + c) / n
)

Do not place spaces around code in parentheses or square brackets (unless there’s a comma, in which case see above).

# Good
if (debug) do(x)
diamonds[5, ]

# Bad
if ( debug ) do(x)  # No spaces around debug
x[1,]   # Needs a space after the comma
x[1 ,]  # Space goes after comma not before

title: Code Etiquette 101
text:
Commenting
Comment your code. Tell me what you’re doing or why you did it. Explain things. If you don’t explain something, I may not know how to grade your assignment. Each line of a comment should begin with the comment symbol and a single space: #.






Tip



Comments should explain the why, not the what.


Use commented lines of - and = to break up your file into easily readable chunks if you’re not coding in a .rmd file.

# Load data ---------------------------

# Plot data ---------------------------

title: Code Etiquette 101
text:
Pipes

Right-hand assignment
In this house, we do not support using right-hand assignment.

# Good
results <- iris %>%
  dplyr::summarize(max_petal = max(Petal.Width))

# Bad
iris %>%
  dplyr::summarize(max_petal = max(Petal.Width)) -> results

This convention differs substantially from practices in other languages and makes it harder to see in code where an object is defined. E.g. searching for foo <- is easier than searching for foo <- and -> foo (possibly split over lines).

title: Code Etiquette 101
Code Etiquette 101
text:
ggplot Do’s and Don’t’s

To Pipe Or To Not Pipe
Styling suggestions for + used to separate ggplot2 layers are very similar to those for |> or %>% in pipelines. Both can be used interchangeably. We will use + and %>% in this course.


White Space
+ should always have a space before it, and should be followed by a new line. This is true even if your plot has only two layers. After the first step, each line should be indented by two spaces.
If you are creating a ggplot off of a dplyr pipeline, there should only be one level of indentation.

# Good
iris %>%
  filter(Species == "setosa") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length)) +
  geom_point()

# Bad
iris %>%
  filter(Species == "setosa") %>%
ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length)) +
geom_point() # this extra indent is not needed

# Bad
iris |>
  filter(Species == "setosa") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length)) + geom_point() # do not put your geom_ call inline!



Long lines
If the arguments to a ggplot2 layer don’t all fit on one line, put each argument on its own line and indent:

# Good
iris %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "Sepal width, in cm",
    y = "Sepal length, in cm",
    title = "Sepal length vs. width of irises"
    )

# Okay
iris %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "Sepal width, in cm", y = "Sepal length, in cm", 
    title = "Sepal length vs. width of irises")

# Bad
iris %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "Sepal width, in cm", y = "Sepal length, in cm", subtitle = "Dot Plot", legend = "Species", title = "Sepal length vs. width of irises") 

ggplot2 allows you to do data manipulation, such as filtering or slicing, within the data argument. Avoid this, and instead do the data manipulation in a pipeline before starting plotting.

# Good
iris %>%
  filter(Species == "setosa") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length)) +
  geom_point()

# Bad
ggplot(filter(iris, Species == "setosa"), aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length)) +
  geom_point()

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
Abalone in a Kelp Forest | Photo Credit: Stefan Andrews / Ocean Image Bank
Welcome! Let’s get excited about coastal marine microbial ecology! This “course” was written and developed with the intrepid, undergraduate ecologist in mind. It makes use of real-world ecological questions using real-world ecological data. This bootcamp was also designed to meet a variety of experience levels for students enrolled in EVE 113.
Some of you may have zero experience using R; Others may be well versed in using the console, tidyverse and ggplot, but may be unfamiliar with things like vegan or unix. It’s completely possible this is your first time ever hearing of R (but hopefully not the last!), or it could be your hundredth. Either way, no matter the situation, this resource is meant to serve you! If there is a problem you’re hoping to tackle that is not included in this guide, please don’t hesitate to notify your TA or instructor. We are here to help you succeed and grow your toolkit as an ecologist.
This guide is setup like a website: We have a home page (the page you’re on now), the Getting Started page, and the Tutorial page(s). You can navigate between each of these by simply heading to the top of the web browser you’re currently viewing this on and clicking on the tabs. This home page won’t contain more than a general overview of how this bootcamp is structured, how to get the most out of your learning, and information for further “R education” beyond EVE 113.

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
Learning Objectives
At the top of every tutorial, there will be numbered learning objectives stating the goals and material covered in the tutorial itself. They will look like:

A series of bullet pointed or objectives for that specific tutorial.
There might be more than one learning objective, or sub-learning objectives.
This numbered list will give you a sneak peek into what the tutorial will be covering.
If you already know how to do some things in the learning objectives then you can consider the tutorial a good chance to review your knowledge.

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
The Actual Tutorial
The tutorials will give you the skills needed to complete your final project/paper. I will try to include screenshots of an actual working RStudio environment where applicable so you can better understand what we’re supposed to be doing. However, most tutorials will largely be made up of “chunks” of code that I expect you to either directly copy/paste into your RStudio or, better yet, type yourself.






Tip



Actually typing the code yourself, rather than copy/pasting will help you better absorb the material and skills you are learning.


Code chunks included in tutorials will looks like this:

ggplot2::ggplot(data = cars, aes(x = speed, y = dist)) +
  geom_point() 

Or maybe even like this:

increment1 <- function(input){
  incremented_value <- input + 1
  return(incremented_value)
}

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
Do It Yourself and “Assignments”
My Do It Yourself (or DIYs) are built-in to provide short breaks between content-heavy topics. These allow you to test and implement the knowledge you gain as you move along in the tutorials. The DIY’s are also the component parts to your assignment(s). This will help me evaluate if you’ve actually completed the learning objectives in the tutorials and are ready to tackle working with our class data.






Do It Yourself T0-00
This is what a DIY will look like. It will include the header above with a number associated with that tutorial. Say, for example, this is DIY 7 in Tutorial 4, the header would look like:
Do It Yourself T4-07
a. Then each DIY may include multiple parts.
b. Each part will be a specific task or question that may ask you to explain a topic, write a line of code, or complete a task.



DIYs are graded for completion, not accuracy. By August 27th you will turn in your DIYs to your TA (me) for feedback. I’ve done this to discourage use of AI so you don’t feel pressured to get “everything correct” but rather focus on the actual acquisition of knowledge. Again, the function of the DIYs is to (a) break up the learning material and (b) test your skills. I will publish answer keys to your DIYs after August 27th and provide feedback accordingly.

Completing Assignments
Navigate to Files on our class Canvas page and find the R Preparedness folder. Download the .rmd file named “R Prep”. At the end of each tutorial, you will knit your .rmd document as an html and submit it via canvas by the due date. More on how to do this in Tutorial 0.
The .rmd will include chunks of code and places for you to answer the DIYs as you work through the tutorial.

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
Review
At the end of every tutorial, there will be a review (and celebration) of all that you’ve accomplished.

If you read the review and can mentally say: “Yes! I know how to answer all of these questions” than congratulations, you’ve completed all the learning objectives!!
You should give yourself a massive pat on the back and do a little celebration when you can do that (it’s a big deal)!
If you find yourself unable to understand a topic in the review, I suggest taking time to review the tutorial again to make sure you are understanding what is expected of you.

I will end every tutorial with a message and a “celebration” photo, and that is how you know you’ve reached the end of the educational content. Hooray!!!

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
Works Cited
At very, very bottom, I will cite any of the packages or resources I used to make the tutorial. When you’re writing up your final report, you may refer back to these tutorials for the citation information to include in your own works cited. Please make sure you’re citing the necessary software you’re using in your work (giving credit where credit is due is very important to avoid plagiarism and promote reproducibility)!

title: Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!
text:
About Me
Hello! I’m Tallulah (the person who wrote these tutorial(s) and built this website for you)! I will be your TA for this course and am here to help you learn how to use R now and in the future. I am a first year ecology PhD student in Dr. Brown’s lab. I graduated from Emory University (which is in Atlanta, Georgia if you’ve never heard of it) with my Bachelor’s of Science in environmental science and biology. I am interested in studying host-microbe-pathogen dynamics in marine invertebrates.
In my research and day-to-day work, I’ve come to use R. Like a lot. I first became passionate about using R for ecology during my junior year when I took a disease ecology course with my research mentor at Emory. In that course I learned how build and implement mechanistic models to study disease transmission in various systems – all of which I did using R. I went on to complete an REU at the California Academy of Sciences where I did computational and statistical research for 10 weeks to serve a conservation breeding program on the critically endangered sunflower sea star! After that summer, I traveled to Australia to work with Dr. David Bourne and Dr. Ira Cooke at the Australia Institute for Marine Science. Dr. Bourne and Cooke are marine microbial biologists/bioninformaticians, so my research involved a lot of bioinformatics work. Like a lot. Now I use R all the time! Yippee!
But, it’s important you know I started out undergrad hating R and statistics and everything to do with data science. Heck, I never wanted to touch R again after my first year stats class. However, once I actually learned how to use R, the appreciation for it as a tool and not my Mortal Enemy quickly blossomed. By the end of this course, I hope I can impart on you some knowledge and skills to make R easy and fun to use now and in the future.

title: Tutorial 0
text:
Cyanobacterial growth in a lake | Photo Credit: Rebecca North


Welcome to your “first” R tutorial! Well, technically this is tutorial 0, but this lesson will form the foundational knowledge you will need to start learning the basics of coding in R! Let’s get started…



Be able to describe and navigate the RStudio computing environment.
Learn how to set your working directory and understand pathing in a computing environment.
Learn how to create new script and .rmd files.
Be able to set evaluations and customization in .rmd code chunks with knitr.
Understand good code etiquette and commenting guidelines, especially with research reproducibility in mind.







Note



You will not be submitting an assignment associated with this tutorial. This is simply meant to provide you with foundational knowledge in order to use R.
If you have extensive experience in R or RStudio, you may proceed on to Tutorial 1.





Start by opening the RStudio application.
If you’re opening RStudio for the first time this might be quite overwhelming. Fear not, this will become more familiar over the course of the next 6 weeks.
For now, let’s break this down as if you were currently working on a project:


At the top of your interface you have your menu and script tab. This is where the data sets and files you’re actively working on live. You can easily navigate between files like you would between tabs on a search engine.
Below these tabs is your code editor. This is where the permanent, written down portions of your code live.
Below the code editor is the Console, Terminal, and Background Jobs windows. The Console is where outputs from your commands are printed (depending on your global chunk options). You can also type commands directly into the Console.







Warning



Typing code directly into the Console will not permanently save any of your work after you close out of your current RStudio session.
I highly recommend conducting all your coding for this course in the code editor rather than in the console.



The far right top window is your Environment - where all your data sets and vectors live. To view any of these in full screen you can simply press on the blue and white play button to the left of the vector name and it will open up in the script tab.
The bottom right of the screen is where Help prompts and Plots can be viewed. You can also view files in your working directory from here by navigating to the Files window.

Now your RStudio won’t look exactly like that above screenshot when you open it for the first time. Instead, it will look more like the screenshot below.
Firstly, direct your attention to the top tab that says Console, Terminal, and Background Jobs.

Again, the Console is where you code directly with R and where outputs to commands will print.
Find the point where your cursor is blinking towards the bottom. The > indicates the Prompt space. This is where you can type your commands directly into the Console.




Now, we’re going to type out our first ever command into the Console! Yay! Go ahead and either manually type or copy/paste the command below then press ‘return’ or ‘Enter’ on your keyboard.

getwd()

Congrats!! You’ve now typed your first command in R. This should result in a response being printed below your command that looks (something) like this:

Above, you asked R to “get my working directory” with the command getwd() by using the starting prompt > in the Console; A response was printed after you hit Enter.
This is lot’s of new vocabulary, so lets zoom it in and break it down.

Here you’ve commanded R to getwd() and it prints your response, above a new prompt line.
This is how R works (for the most part). You type a command in the Console (or in a R chunk) and it will either:

Print a response for you in the Console.
Open file into the script tab.
Open a plot on the bottom right.
Open the help window.

As you can see, when I run the command getwd(), it tells me that my current working directory is a general space on my computer called /Users/loganstory. What this really means is that when I try to read new files into R, or “write” files out of R, it will assume that I want to put them in this general folder.






Tip



As a good practice, getting and setting your working directory is one of the first things you want to do when starting a new project, document, or script file. Doing this tells R where to locate and save files as your work on your code.


There are two methods to set your working directory…


In order to change your working directory manually, we will use the setwd() command.
For example, if I wanted to change my working directory to an existing folder called R-tutorials located on my Desktop in a folder called EVE 113, I’d run the following code:

setwd("/Users/yourusernamehere/Desktop/EVE 113/R tutorials")

This is the directory path R will now use when searching and saving files as I work: Users > yourusenamehere > Desktop > EVE 113 > R tutorials. The “>” indicates the directionality that R is searching in my computer for the works pace.



The second option to set your working directory is the point-and-click approach (I personally prefer setting my directory manually, but others might like this better).
To do this:

Go to Session in the menu.
Select Set Working Directory.
Choose Choose Directory… and navigate to the desired folder on your computer.






For the sake of this course (and these tutorials), I’d like you to make a folder to set as your working directory before we proceed any further. You can use the following template if you’d like to keep things simple:

Create a primary folder on your desktop called EVE 113.
Create a sub-folder inside EVE 113 called Coding Tutorials – (this will let you keep coding tutorial work and class coding work separate from one another).
Create another sub-folder called Tutorial 0.

The path you’ve just created will be something along the lines of:
/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 0
A file path tells you (and your computer) the exact place you can locate a file. Paths can either be absolute or relative. You use different paths, in different scenarios, to set your working directory.
An absolute path would be something like…

setwd("/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 0") # on macOS

or

setwd("C:\Users\yourusername\Desktop\EVE 113\Coding Tutorials\Tutorial 0") # on Windows


Here the working directory is explicitly specified (/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials).
It includes the set destination for a file (/Tutorial 0, or essentially where you want to go with the path).

A relative path would be something like…

setwd("./Tutorial 0")


Here, the working directory is implicitly specified.
A . before the forward or backslash indicates you are moving down the path, whereas .. indicates movement up the path.

This idea is illustrated here:

Understanding the path concept will help you when saving, accessing, and moving around your internal operating system. If you encounter problems setting your working directory, try restarting the RStudio application before seeking further assistance.



A .rmd (or Rmarkdown) file is a type of R script file that allows you to provide detailed descriptions of your code, analyses, and even interpretation.
To make a new .rmd file, go through the following steps:

Set your working directory.

setwd("/Users/yourusenamehere/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 0")

Check that you’ve successfully changed your working directory.

getwd()

Proceed to the top left corner and click the New File icon.
Scroll down and select R Markdown…

When the document window pops up, title your new .rmd “Tutorial 0”.
Select your Default Output Format. This will be what your file will be saved as when you Knit your .rmd. Any format will do for the sake of this tutorial, but I often prefer HTML.







Note



In future tutorials you will be completing your assignments in a pre-prepared .rmd file. This output option will already be chosen for you in those files.



Click Okay.

Your .rmd document should be created! There’s already a lot of text included in the base Rmarkdown document, but some of it should help with getting oriented. Go ahead and take a minute to explore.

Start by pressing the green play button (which is the Run Current Chunk button) to the right corner of Chunk 1 (which begins at line 8).






Tip



You can also run individual lines of code by pressing the ‘control ^’ and ‘return’ keys on your keyboard at the same time.
If you want to run the entire chunk of code at once, you can use the Run Current Chunk button.


The line knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE) in code chunk 1 is used in Rmarkdown documents to globally configure code chunks so that the underlying source code is visible in the final rendered output.
Here, you’re using the package knitr:: to set any outputs you run in Rmarkdown to be included when you knit the final document. This code chunk is automatically included in all .rmd files you create unless you personalize your RStudio otherwise. We won’t worry about this too much for now.
Scroll down to the second code chunk.
Run chunk 2. You should see a new output print directly below your code chunk inside the code editor AND the Console.

summary(cars)

     speed           dist       
 Min.   : 4.0   Min.   :  2.00  
 1st Qu.:12.0   1st Qu.: 26.00  
 Median :15.0   Median : 36.00  
 Mean   :15.4   Mean   : 42.98  
 3rd Qu.:19.0   3rd Qu.: 56.00  
 Max.   :25.0   Max.   :120.00  


This is the output of the command summary(cars).

Here, R is providing a summary() of the data set cars.
Now you may be asking yourself, “where did this data set come from??? I don’t have any data sets in my computer called cars.” You’d be 100% correct!
You don’t have a data set called cars on your computer, but RStudio does. This is one of the “native” data sets included in the R and Rstudio computing environment. These can be used to practice data manipulation and visualization and we will use a few of these throughout these tutorials.
The data gives the speed of cars and the distances taken to stop. The data was recorded in the 1920s. cars is data frame with 50 observations on 2 variables.



Variable
Variable Type
Unit


speed
numeric
Speed (mph)


dist
numeric
Stopping distance (ft)



We will use cars in this tutorial to explore adding code chunks and running individual lines of code.
In code chunk 3, there is code to produce a plot. This also makes use of another internal data set called pressure, which is a 2 variable data set exploring the relation between temperature in degrees Celsius and vapor pressure of mercury in millimeters (of mercury).
Run this code chunk and scroll down to look at the output.

plot(pressure)









You might be quick to notice that this plot output is very large, and you have to scroll far down to even be able to view the entire thing in the code editor. As we progress in this course, we will continue adding multiple lines of code per code chunk and constantly scrolling up and down will become very tiresome very quickly.
So, whenever we generate a plot or need a summary from our code chunk output, navigating the printed results in the code editor will become cumbersome. For the purposes of continuity in this class, we will be setting our code chunk outputs to be in the Console instead of inline.



Click the wheel icon  (a.k.a. the Format Options button) below the file tab bar (directly to the right of the knit button).
Scroll down and select Chunk Output in Console.
RStudio will then ask you if you’d like to remove any inline chunk output you’ve already generated. Click Remove Output.
Run Chunk 3 again. If you did this correctly, you should see the following: 

This generates your plots in the Plot window at the bottom right of your screen. Here you can navigate between Files, Plots, and other environmental viewers. Keep the plot tab open for now.
If you scroll back up to the top of your document you should now see the following at the top:

---
title: "Tutorial 0"
author: "You Name Here"
date: "Todays Date Here"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

You can manually change the chunk output type by simply adding this option at the top of your .rmd manually, like above, but it’s significantly easier to just do the point-and-click method.




Adding comments on your code is by far one of the most important data management practices you’ll develop as a scientist. Some code doesn’t inherently call for comments, but it’s good to get into the habit of adding frequent, descriptive comments to your code early on.
The more comments, the better (in my opinion, there is not such thing as too many comments on your code).
In Rmarkdown, you can also add more detailed text above and below code chunks (like the text included in our new .rmd file we’ve created). In this case, the text in our new document introduces you to Rmarkdown, but in the future we will use this for interpretative comments about our work.
With the code chunks we already have, lets add some comments. Go ahead and add the following comment next to summary(cars) then run the chunk.

summary(cars) <- gives a summary of the native data set cars

…that doesn’t work. When you run this, you should receive the following error:

Error: unexpected symbol in “summary(cars) <- gives a”

This means your code is not able to run because R encountered an unexpected symbol in the text you added to the chunk. That’s because <- is an actual argument in the R programming language.
Additionally, there is no such command as gives in R.
The moment R recognizes something wrong in your code, it won’t run and it will return an error or a warning message.






Tip



It is always best practice to ensure all of your code runs without any errors or warnings before submitting it.
A good way to check if your code is running completely without errors or warnings is by scrolling all the way to the bottom of your .rmd and running all the chunks above it. Everything should run synchronously.


So, how can we add a comment to our code if we can’t do it that way?? Let’s try something else:

summary(cars) "gives a summary of the native data set cars"

Huh… Using quotes around our comment doesn’t work either?? When you do this, you’ll receive the error message:

Error: unexpected string constant in “summary(cars)”gives a summary of the native data set cars””

That is because R is not able to recognize the constraint "gives a summary of the native data set cars" as a part of the code evaluation summary(cars).
Let’s try one more thing… Go ahead and edit chunk 2 with the following:

summary(cars) # gives a summary of the native data set cars

Yay!!! It works!!!
Using a preemptive # before your comment text will turn your comment a different color and R will ignore any text included to the right of it.
If you press enter to a new line of code, you will need to add another # before any additional comments you make. Essentially, what R is doing is it’s reading what you’ve typed into any code chunk left to right. When it encounters a #, it reads past it until a new line of code begins.



At the end of the document on line 32 (or 33 if you’ve been completing the DIYs as we’ve progressed) there is the following text explaining the code chunk options.






Note



The echo = FALSE parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.


RMarkdown parameters (or chunk options) allow you to alter individual chunks of code to alter the code output in some manner when you knit your final document.
For the purposes of this course, we will only really care about eval, include, and echo, but know that many (many) more chunk options exist. To find more ways to edit or alter you code chunks, visit this cheat sheet. Chunk options come with an operative argument (like eval) and a conditional statement (like TRUE or FALSE).
Here’s a quick guide to what these mean below:







Option
Function




eval
dictates whether a code block is actually executed when you render, knit, or compile your document.


include
controls the visibility of a code chunk’s output in your final rendered document.


echo
controls whether the source code itself appears in your final rendered document. echo = numeric will select specific lines of code to display (e.g., echo = 1:2 displays source code lines 1 through 2).


warning
hides or shows package and system warning messages. TRUE (Default) displays warning boxes.


error
configures how the document compilation responds to failing code. FALSE (Default) halts compilation immediately if a code error occurs.


message
suppresses text notifications like library startup alerts. TRUE (Default) shows messages associated with your code.


cache
stores code chunk results to optimize future document rendering. FALSE (Default) will re-run every line of code every time you compile or knit your document.









Note



Default chunk options are usually TRUE (unless I’ve specified otherwise above).


To add a chunk option you should:

Place your cursor directly into the code brackets after the r:


```{r}


Add a comma after r.


```{r, }


Add a chunk option.


```{r, echo=TRUE}


To add multiple chunk options, simply add a comma after every option.


```{r, echo=TRUE, include=FALSE}




Now to add your very own chunk of R code into your new .rmd file!!

Make some space at the bottom of your current .rmd file. One or two lines should be good.
Select the Insert a new code chunk  icon in the menu.
Select Insert a new R chunk. 


You should now have your very own chunk of R code ready for use.



Finally, to save your RMarkdown document, navigate to the menu and press the ‘Save’ icon.

If you set your working directory correctly at the beginning of this tutorial, you will be directed to save your Untitled.rmd in the folder you set as your working directory

(e.g., /Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 1).

Now, when we turn in assignments in this course, you will need to knit your code to save it as the output type you set at the beginning of this tutorial when you first made the Rmarkdown file.

Locate the Knit icon (which is next to the Settings wheel and literally looks like a little ball of kitting yarn).
Click Knit. 
You will be redirected to the knitted version of your code.
In this case, I set my output type as HTML, so it will redirect me to a HTML file including all of the text and configured code chunks within the original .rmd file. It (should) look something like this:





Wowie! That was a lot! If you’ve made it to the end of this, CONGRATS! You now know how to start coding in RStudio. Give yourself a HUGE pat on the back because learning the basics to R and Rmarkdown are by far the hardest parts of learning how to code in R.
However, I’m confident these essentials will give you the foundational knowledge you will need to succeed in this course. And I can (hopefully) promise once you master these skills, learning how to use RStudio to your advantage will only get easier from here.
To review, you should be able to:

Navigate the RStudio computing environment, including describing where the Console is.
Set a working directory and explain the difference between an absolute and relative path.
Create, edit, and save Rmarkdown files.
Edit chunk options in a Rmarkdown file.
Understand how to comment on your code.
Provide a summary on a data set.
Plot a simple, 2 variable data set.

If you find you are unable to do one or more of the above tasks, go ahead and take some time to review this tutorial again until it starts to make sense. Happy coding!

title: Tutorial 0
text:
Be able to describe and navigate the RStudio computing environment.
Learn how to set your working directory and understand pathing in a computing environment.
Learn how to create new script and .rmd files.
Be able to set evaluations and customization in .rmd code chunks with knitr.
Understand good code etiquette and commenting guidelines, especially with research reproducibility in mind.







Note



You will not be submitting an assignment associated with this tutorial. This is simply meant to provide you with foundational knowledge in order to use R.
If you have extensive experience in R or RStudio, you may proceed on to Tutorial 1.

title: Tutorial 0
text:
Start by opening the RStudio application.
If you’re opening RStudio for the first time this might be quite overwhelming. Fear not, this will become more familiar over the course of the next 6 weeks.
For now, let’s break this down as if you were currently working on a project:


At the top of your interface you have your menu and script tab. This is where the data sets and files you’re actively working on live. You can easily navigate between files like you would between tabs on a search engine.
Below these tabs is your code editor. This is where the permanent, written down portions of your code live.
Below the code editor is the Console, Terminal, and Background Jobs windows. The Console is where outputs from your commands are printed (depending on your global chunk options). You can also type commands directly into the Console.







Warning



Typing code directly into the Console will not permanently save any of your work after you close out of your current RStudio session.
I highly recommend conducting all your coding for this course in the code editor rather than in the console.



The far right top window is your Environment - where all your data sets and vectors live. To view any of these in full screen you can simply press on the blue and white play button to the left of the vector name and it will open up in the script tab.
The bottom right of the screen is where Help prompts and Plots can be viewed. You can also view files in your working directory from here by navigating to the Files window.

Now your RStudio won’t look exactly like that above screenshot when you open it for the first time. Instead, it will look more like the screenshot below.
Firstly, direct your attention to the top tab that says Console, Terminal, and Background Jobs.

Again, the Console is where you code directly with R and where outputs to commands will print.
Find the point where your cursor is blinking towards the bottom. The > indicates the Prompt space. This is where you can type your commands directly into the Console.

title: Tutorial 0
text:
Now, we’re going to type out our first ever command into the Console! Yay! Go ahead and either manually type or copy/paste the command below then press ‘return’ or ‘Enter’ on your keyboard.

getwd()

Congrats!! You’ve now typed your first command in R. This should result in a response being printed below your command that looks (something) like this:

Above, you asked R to “get my working directory” with the command getwd() by using the starting prompt > in the Console; A response was printed after you hit Enter.
This is lot’s of new vocabulary, so lets zoom it in and break it down.

Here you’ve commanded R to getwd() and it prints your response, above a new prompt line.
This is how R works (for the most part). You type a command in the Console (or in a R chunk) and it will either:

Print a response for you in the Console.
Open file into the script tab.
Open a plot on the bottom right.
Open the help window.

As you can see, when I run the command getwd(), it tells me that my current working directory is a general space on my computer called /Users/loganstory. What this really means is that when I try to read new files into R, or “write” files out of R, it will assume that I want to put them in this general folder.






Tip



As a good practice, getting and setting your working directory is one of the first things you want to do when starting a new project, document, or script file. Doing this tells R where to locate and save files as your work on your code.


There are two methods to set your working directory…


In order to change your working directory manually, we will use the setwd() command.
For example, if I wanted to change my working directory to an existing folder called R-tutorials located on my Desktop in a folder called EVE 113, I’d run the following code:

setwd("/Users/yourusernamehere/Desktop/EVE 113/R tutorials")

This is the directory path R will now use when searching and saving files as I work: Users > yourusenamehere > Desktop > EVE 113 > R tutorials. The “>” indicates the directionality that R is searching in my computer for the works pace.



The second option to set your working directory is the point-and-click approach (I personally prefer setting my directory manually, but others might like this better).
To do this:

Go to Session in the menu.
Select Set Working Directory.
Choose Choose Directory… and navigate to the desired folder on your computer.

title: Tutorial 0
text:
For the sake of this course (and these tutorials), I’d like you to make a folder to set as your working directory before we proceed any further. You can use the following template if you’d like to keep things simple:

Create a primary folder on your desktop called EVE 113.
Create a sub-folder inside EVE 113 called Coding Tutorials – (this will let you keep coding tutorial work and class coding work separate from one another).
Create another sub-folder called Tutorial 0.

The path you’ve just created will be something along the lines of:
/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 0
A file path tells you (and your computer) the exact place you can locate a file. Paths can either be absolute or relative. You use different paths, in different scenarios, to set your working directory.
An absolute path would be something like…

setwd("/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 0") # on macOS

or

setwd("C:\Users\yourusername\Desktop\EVE 113\Coding Tutorials\Tutorial 0") # on Windows


Here the working directory is explicitly specified (/Users/yourusername/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials).
It includes the set destination for a file (/Tutorial 0, or essentially where you want to go with the path).

A relative path would be something like…

setwd("./Tutorial 0")


Here, the working directory is implicitly specified.
A . before the forward or backslash indicates you are moving down the path, whereas .. indicates movement up the path.

This idea is illustrated here:

Understanding the path concept will help you when saving, accessing, and moving around your internal operating system. If you encounter problems setting your working directory, try restarting the RStudio application before seeking further assistance.

A .rmd (or Rmarkdown) file is a type of R script file that allows you to provide detailed descriptions of your code, analyses, and even interpretation.
To make a new .rmd file, go through the following steps:

Set your working directory.

setwd("/Users/yourusenamehere/Desktop/EVE 113/Coding Tutorials/Tutorial 0")

Check that you’ve successfully changed your working directory.

getwd()

Proceed to the top left corner and click the New File icon.
Scroll down and select R Markdown…

When the document window pops up, title your new .rmd “Tutorial 0”.
Select your Default Output Format. This will be what your file will be saved as when you Knit your .rmd. Any format will do for the sake of this tutorial, but I often prefer HTML.







Note



In future tutorials you will be completing your assignments in a pre-prepared .rmd file. This output option will already be chosen for you in those files.



Click Okay.

Tip

If you want to run the entire chunk of code at once, you can use the Run Current Chunk button.

Here, you’re using the package knitr:: to set any outputs you run in Rmarkdown to be included when you knit the final document. This code chunk is automatically included in all .rmd files you create unless you personalize your RStudio otherwise. We won’t worry about this too much for now.
Scroll down to the second code chunk.
Run chunk 2. You should see a new output print directly below your code chunk inside the code editor AND the Console.

summary(cars)

This is the output of the command summary(cars).

Here, R is providing a summary() of the data set cars.
Now you may be asking yourself, “where did this data set come from??? I don’t have any data sets in my computer called cars.” You’d be 100% correct!
You don’t have a data set called cars on your computer, but RStudio does. This is one of the “native” data sets included in the R and Rstudio computing environment. These can be used to practice data manipulation and visualization and we will use a few of these throughout these tutorials.
The data gives the speed of cars and the distances taken to stop. The data was recorded in the 1920s. cars is data frame with 50 observations on 2 variables.



Variable
Variable Type
Unit


speed
numeric
Speed (mph)


dist
numeric
Stopping distance (ft)



We will use cars in this tutorial to explore adding code chunks and running individual lines of code.
In code chunk 3, there is code to produce a plot. This also makes use of another internal data set called pressure, which is a 2 variable data set exploring the relation between temperature in degrees Celsius and vapor pressure of mercury in millimeters (of mercury).
Run this code chunk and scroll down to look at the output.

plot(pressure)

Scroll down and select Chunk Output in Console.
RStudio will then ask you if you’d like to remove any inline chunk output you’ve already generated. Click Remove Output.
Run Chunk 3 again. If you did this correctly, you should see the following: 

This generates your plots in the Plot window at the bottom right of your screen. Here you can navigate between Files, Plots, and other environmental viewers. Keep the plot tab open for now.
If you scroll back up to the top of your document you should now see the following at the top:

  chunk_output_type: console
